title: Coastal Proximity Analysis (CPA) Method
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cchj1","text":"Cartographic interpretations of coastal proximity often rely on simple Euclidean distance, which fails to account for the physical barriers and connections that shape human interaction with the landscape. As a result, two places both described as ‚Äútwo kilometres from the sea‚Äù may exhibit vastly different degrees of accessibility when considering the realities of terrain-based mobility. This method addresses this limitation by generating a numerical value for time travelled, using Tobler‚Äôs Hiking Function (THF), to quantify the spatial relationship between a place of habitation and its nearest coastline. THF is the optimal choice for this analysis because it models embodied movement (the time and energy required for a person to traverse terrain on foot) rather than abstract distance. Unlike modern cost functions, which may incorporate anachronistic modes of transport (e.g., vehicles or modern roads), THF is grounded in empirical observations of human walking speeds across varying slopes. It accounts for the physiological effort of ascending and descending terrain, providing a realistic estimate of travel time that reflects the physical capabilities and experiences of past societies. By focusing on pedestrian movement, this method ensures that coastal proximity values accurately represent the challenges and opportunities presented by the landscape to historical or prehistoric communities. Beyond archaeology, this approach offers a replicable framework for assessing the spatial proximity of any location to the coastline, making it a versatile tool for spatial analysis in diverse contexts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":485,"length":24}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

Download: https://qgis.org/download/","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f8hq3","text":"- Digital Elevation Model (DEM): any type will work, as long as it contains an area that covers coastline. For this analysis, EU-DEM was used, though more accurate COP-DEM is now available at 90, 30 and 10 m spatial resolution, depending on account access: https://dataspace.copernicus.eu/explore-data/data-collections/copernicus-contributing-missions/collections-description/COP-DEM","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lhtdg","text":"- Plugin: ‚ÄúLeast Cost Path‚Äù plugin for QGIS: https://plugins.qgis.org/plugins/leastcostpath/. Install through the QGIS plugin interface (Plugins/Manage and Install Plugins), searching for ‚ÄúLeast Cost Path‚Äù. Select ‚ÄúInstall Plugin‚Äù.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zpktg","text":"- Data: Point data for at least one archaeological site in CSV format. Typical good practice will include four columns, including ‚ÄúID‚Äù, ‚ÄúName‚Äù, ‚ÄúX‚Äù and ‚ÄúY‚Äù data.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Open the QGIS program and create a new project (Project‚ÜíNew).

First ensure that your Project CRS is initially set to EPSG:4326. This can be found in the bottom right of the screen and will display EPSG and a numerical value. Once selected, click ‚ÄúApply‚Äù and then ‚ÄúOK‚Äù.

Add your DEM (henceforth as ‚ÄúYour_DEM‚Äù) into QGIS (Layer‚ÜíAdd Layer‚ÜíAdd Raster Layer). This will open up a dialogue box. Select the three dots in ‚ÄúSource‚Äù (‚Ä¶) and navigate to your file path. Once selected, click ‚ÄúAdd‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù.

Load your CSV (henceforth ‚ÄúYour_CSV‚Äù) point data (Layer‚ÜíAdd Layer‚ÜíAdd Delimited Text Layer). This will open up a dialogue box. Select the three dots in ‚ÄúSource‚Äù (‚Ä¶) and navigate to your file path. When importing, ensure that the ‚ÄúX‚Äù and ‚ÄúY‚Äù axes are correctly configured in the dialogue box. Once selected, click ‚ÄúAdd‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù.

Convert ‚ÄúYour_CSV‚Äù into a Geopackage in the EPSG:4326 CRS. Right-click on ‚ÄúYour_CSV‚Äù and save as a Geopackage (Export‚ÜíSave Features As). In the dialogue box, ensure Geopackage is selected from the format. Select the three dots (‚Ä¶) in ‚ÄúFile Name‚Äù and name your Geopackage ‚ÄúYour_Data‚Äù. For the ‚ÄúLayer Name‚Äù, type ‚ÄúPoints_4326‚Äù. Leave the layer in the EPSG:4326 projection. Tick ‚ÄúAdd saved file to map‚Äù and then click ‚ÄúOK‚Äù.
Ensure that your sites are appearing in the correct position.

Reproject the ‚ÄúYour_DEM‚Äù (Raster‚ÜíProjections‚ÜíWarp [Reproject]) onto an appropriate Transverse Mercator CRS based on your location. This will project your DEM in metres. As your input layer, set it to ‚ÄúYour_DEM.‚Äù Select your desired ‚ÄúTarget CRS‚Äù. For example, as a researcher working in Greece, I set to: EPSG: 2100/Greek Grid. This step is essential. Leave the ‚ÄúResampling method to use‚Äù as ‚ÄúNearest Neighbour‚Äù. Under ‚ÄúReprojected‚Äù, select the three dots (‚Ä¶) and save the file to your path of choice (hereafter ‚ÄúReprojected_DEM). Click ‚ÄúRun‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù. Note: Geographic Coordinate Systems, such as EPSG:4326, use angular units (degrees) to represent locations on the Earth's surface. These units are not suitable for measuring distances directly, as the length of a degree of latitude or longitude varies depending on location. A projected CRS, such as Transverse Mercator, converts these angular units into linear units (metres), enabling accurate distance and area calculations.

Convert ‚ÄúPoints_4326‚Äù layer into a new layer (Vector‚ÜíData Management Tools‚ÜíReproject Layer) in the chosen Transverse mercator CRS (same as DEM). Under ‚ÄúReprojected‚Äù, select the three dots (‚Ä¶) and select your original Geopackage ‚ÄúYour_Data‚Äù. Click ‚ÄúRun‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù. In the dialogue box that will emerge for the ‚ÄúLayer Name‚Äù, save the layer as ‚ÄúReprojected_Points‚Äù.
Ensure that your sites are appearing in the correct position.
Now set your project CRS to your chosen Transverse Mercator CRS in metres (See also Point 2).
Download the ‚ÄúLeast Cost Path‚Äù plugin in QGIS.

Load the dialogue for the Contour (Raster‚ÜíExtraction‚ÜíContours) function. Set the ‚ÄúInput layer‚Äù to your DEM ‚ÄúProjected_DEM‚Äù and make sure to select the ‚ÄúBand Number‚Äù (there should be one option). Set the ‚ÄúOffset from zero relative to which to interpret intervals‚Äù to 1.00 unit (1 m). To save computational power, set the ‚ÄúInterval between contour lines‚Äù to 10000.00 units (10000 m). This ensures that only the 1 m contour line (representing sea level) is generated, reducing computational load. To save the file, select the three dots (‚Ä¶) and select your main Geopackage (‚ÄúYour_data‚Äù) and save the layer name as ‚ÄúContours‚Äù. Click ‚ÄúRun‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù. The contour output should now show only the 1 m contour, i.e. sea-level. Inspect the output to ensure the contour accurately represents the 1 m elevation (sea level).

From your ‚ÄúContours‚Äù layer (which is in polyline format), convert the layer to a series of points using (Vector‚ÜíGeometry Tools‚ÜíExtract Vertices) with your ‚ÄúContours‚Äù as the input. To save the file, select the three dots (‚Ä¶) and select the path. Save the file as ‚ÄúCoast_points‚Äù. Click ‚ÄúRun‚Äù and then ‚ÄúClose‚Äù. Inspect the point layer and remove any erroneous or undesired locations from the file. Select ‚ÄúToggle editing‚Äù then ‚ÄúSelect Features by Area or by Single Click‚Äù and use the delete key to delete the point. Note: You can select and delete large areas by dragging a box over the undesired points. This can reduce the file size and computational time significantly for the following Least Cost Path analysis.

Calculate slope using (Raster‚ÜíAnalysis‚ÜíSlope) ‚ÄúProjected_DEM‚Äù as the ‚ÄúInput layer‚Äù. Under ‚ÄúBand number‚Äù select the only option. This can be left as a temporary file (typically named ‚ÄúSlope‚Äù) as this will mean that the following equation need not be edited.

To apply Tobler's hiking function, do the following: enter Raster Calculator (Raster‚ÜíRaster Calculator) and add the following calculation (including brackets): [30 / 1000) / (6 * 2.71828 ^ (ABS(tan(("Slope@1"*3.14159) / 180) + 0.05)))] Note: if you have named your slope raster something other than ‚ÄúSlope‚Äù, add the name to replace this part of the equation: ‚ÄúSlope@1‚Äù. Under ‚ÄúResult Layer‚Äù, click the three dots (‚Ä¶) next to ‚ÄúOutput Layer‚Äù and select your chosen file path. This will create a Tobler‚Äôs hiking function cost surface which you can save as (‚ÄúSave Features As‚Äù) ‚ÄúCost_Surface‚Äù.

Open the dialogue box for the‚ÄúLeast Cost Path‚Äù plugin in QGIS (Processing Toolbox‚ÜíCost distance analysis‚ÜíLeast Cost Path). This can be found in the Processing Toolbox. Select the ‚ÄúCost_Surface‚Äù as the cost raster. Ensure that you also select the raster band (typically there is only one option).

Enable ‚Äúbatch processing‚Äù to create individual Least Cost Paths (LCP) for each site in your point file (if more than one). This can be found to the right of the point input and looks like a green curved arrow (Add image). This automates the process of calculating the LCPs.

Use the ‚ÄúCoast_points‚Äù layer as endpoints and enable the ‚ÄúOnly connect with nearest end points‚Äù option. These can be left as temporary outputs. To execute the analysis, click ‚ÄúRun‚Äù. Depending on the size of your rasters and the points involved in the calculation, the computing time can vary. Note: processing time can be reduced significantly by clipping the ‚ÄúCost_Surface‚Äù to just the area of interest (e.g. island, region etc.). Older computers may take longer to complete the analysis, especially if there are a high number of input points.
The resulting shapefile(s) can be inspected for a value listed under ‚ÄúTotal Cost‚Äù (Right-click layer‚ÜíOpen Attribute Table). This is the cost of the movement based on the Cost Surface.
Copy the ‚ÄúTotal Cost‚Äù value into your database processor (i.e. Excel, Calc) of choice.

Convert from hours to minutes by multiplying the value by 60. This is now the Coastal Proximity Value (CPV) in minutes. Use the CPV to quantify the temporal and spatial relationship between a site and the ancient shoreline.
With this data, you can identify periods of greater or lesser coastal proximity in settlement patterns.
Check for zero slope cells to avoid misleading cost values.
Validate results with ethnographic or historical route data if possible.
Make sure to remove false positive inland areas that may have an elevation of ‚Äú1 m‚Äù. See also ‚ÄúB. Shoreline Delineation.‚Äù
Tabulate the median or average time-cost for sites within different chronological phases to check on larger-scale patterns in Coastal Proximity (see e.g. Nuttall 2024a; 2024b).
creator: Christopher Nuttall
